President Trump has paused the US military escort operation 'Project Freedom' in the Strait of Hormuz, citing diplomatic progress toward a potential deal with Iran. This comes as Iran seized two cargo ships in the strait and continues to threaten commercial shipping.
The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed to commercial shipping, creating the largest global oil supply disruption since the 1970s energy crisis.

The Tigray People's Liberation Front (TPLF) has restored its pre-war government structure in northern Ethiopia, creating rival political administrations and threatening to reignite the devastating civil war that killed hundreds of thousands from 2020-2022.

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio has proposed a UN Security Council resolution demanding Iran cease attacks in the Strait of Hormuz, calling it a 'test of the utility of the United Nations.' The draft resolution could lead to sanctions or potentially authorize force if Iran fails to comply.

Construction on the 2025 sewer/storm pipe rehabilitation project begins Monday, May 4. The $934K CIPP (Cured-in-Place Pipe) program will replace aging mains on 242nd St SW, 5th Ave S, Bowdoin Way, 88th Ave W and ten other segments.

triadic_ontology.findings: Two recent papers advance triadic-relational metaphysics. Christopher Isabelle’s “Triadic Relational Ontology” argues that after the primordial separation of “here/not-here,” intelligibility demands an irreducible third relational field sustained by active tension between poles; dyadic language alone cannot recover this structure. Damiano Migliorini’s “Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics” proposes a speculative trinitarian model in which relationality and multiplicity are transcendental features of being, integrating quantum-structural realism with analytic philosophy of religion to move beyond substance metaphysics.

ai_systems.findings: Three 2026 arXiv papers showcase next-gen autonomous research agents. MARS introduces Budget-Aware MCTS and Reflective Memory to cut compute costs while discovering cross-task insights. Mimosa presents an evolving multi-agent framework that auto-synthesizes and refines scientific workflows via MCP tool discovery, scoring 43.1% on ScienceAgentBench. Finally, Cmbagent demonstrates a semi-autonomous astrophysics pipeline that, with human guidance, won the FAIR Universe weak-lensing challenge, proving hybrid human-agent systems can outperform experts.

weather: Clear start to the day at 54°F, rising to a pleasant 63-64°F under partly sunny skies. Light south-southwest winds 3-10 mph. Only a 10-14% chance of a brief sprinkle. Evening turns mostly cloudy, cooling to the low 50s with light northerly breezes—perfect for a stroll or outdoor dinner without the need for an umbrella.

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council voted 5-1 on Tuesday (May 5) to postpone confirmation of Mayor Mike Rosen’s nominee Stephanie Lucash as the new city administrator until next week’s meeting. Council President Michelle Dotsch said the “very high-level and high-profile position” deserved more public and council scrutiny. If confirmed, Lucash—currently Kenmore’s deputy city manager—will earn an annual base salary of $226,068 for 2026. Separately, the council deadlocked 3-3 on whether to “unpause” the volunteer Salary Commission; Mayor Rosen broke the tie, sending the item to next week’s consent agenda. The commission would set binding pay for elected officials for the 2027-2028 biennium. Planning Director Mike Clugston reported that 15 detached-accessory-dwelling-unit (DADU) permits were issued in 20 [TRUNCATED]

At its April 28 meeting the council formally adopted Edmonds’ first-ever Comprehensive Safety Action Plan, a $300,000 roadmap funded by a federal Safe Streets grant. The plan identifies 11 priority traffic-safety projects citywide—ranging from Highway 99 corridor improvements to downtown-core intersection upgrades—positioning the city to compete for future implementation grants. Annual reports from the city’s contract prosecutor (Walls Law Group) and the Snohomish County Public Defender Association were also presented; the prosecutor noted a new attorney has eliminated last year’s case backlog, while the public defender reported misdemeanor theft (median loss $37.66) remains the top charge in Edmonds.

Police Chief Loi Dawkins updated the council on the department’s progress toward accessing $210,000 in new public-safety sales-tax revenue. Edmonds has collected the 0.1% tax since February, but state rules require six policy updates by June 30: alignment with the Keep Washington Working Act, revised use-of-force policies, enhanced use-of-force data reporting, firearms-relinquishment procedures, related training compliance, and overall state-mandated training standards. Dawkins said EPD is “100 % compliant” with control-tactics training and expects to meet the deadline, after which the city can spend the earmarked funds.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has concluded auditions for the 2025-26 season, but is already preparing for next year. Email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the 2026-27 audition mailing list; all voice parts are heard each August at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Ave). Requirements: a two-minute prepared solo, sight-reading two short choral excerpts in quartet, and ear-repeating a phrase. Tuesday 6:30-9:30 pm rehearsals, $75 annual dues, full COVID vaccination required. Small ensembles Vox, Chroma, and Orpheon hold additional auditions after the main choir starts.
family.toddler: Sno-Isle Libraries has released its May 2026 calendars. Edmonds Library (650 Main St) continues toddler storytimes on Tuesday and Thursday mornings at 10:30 am, plus a Saturday family storytime at 11 am. Lynnwood Library offers Baby Storytime on Wednesdays at 10:30 am and Toddler Storytime on Fridays at 10:30 am. Both locations add special events: Lynnwood hosts "Incredible Arthropods with The Bug Chicks" on May 7 at 4 pm, while Edmonds presents a drop-in art-making day with storytime on May 16. All programs are free and first-come, first-served.

global_news.findings: President Trump has paused the US military escort operation 'Project Freedom' in the Strait of Hormuz, citing diplomatic progress toward a potential deal with Iran. This comes as Iran seized two cargo ships in the strait and continues to threaten commercial shipping. The move represents a significant de-escalation effort after weeks of heightened tensions that have driven global oil prices past $100 per barrel.

The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed to commercial shipping, creating the largest global oil supply disruption since the 1970s energy crisis. The EU has approved a €90 billion loan for Ukraine as the Druzhba pipeline resumes operations, ending months of deadlock over oil supplies to Hungary. Oil prices have eased slightly following Trump's announcement of paused military operations.

The Tigray People's Liberation Front (TPLF) has restored its pre-war government structure in northern Ethiopia, creating rival political administrations and threatening to reignite the devastating civil war that killed hundreds of thousands from 2020-2022. The move violates key provisions of the Pretoria peace agreement and has prompted warnings of 'catastrophic conflict' from the Ethiopian government.

Reform UK leader Nigel Farage faces scrutiny over a £5 million gift from billionaire donor Christopher Harborne that he failed to declare before becoming an MP. Farage claims the money was for personal security and argues there was 'no obligation' to declare it as it was an unconditional, non-political gift. The Conservatives have referred the matter to the parliamentary standards commissioner.

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio has proposed a UN Security Council resolution demanding Iran cease attacks in the Strait of Hormuz, calling it a 'test of the utility of the United Nations.' The draft resolution could lead to sanctions or potentially authorize force if Iran fails to comply. Russia and China previously vetoed similar measures, but the US hopes adjusted language will secure passage.

intellectual_journals.findings: Medieval art transforms the human body into a political and theological battleground, as seen in the 13th-century mosaics of Saint Mark’s Basilica. The cycle depicting Saint Mark’s life—his arrest, dragging, and martyrdom—does not dramatize pain through facial contortion but instead stages a silent contest between violence and composure. Mark’s body remains serene while his tormentors strain, a visual grammar that turns physical suffering into proof of spiritual mastery. A Black executioner appears as the agent of violence, embedding racial hierarchy into sacred narrative. Repeated across medi [TRUNCATED]

Tim Judah reports from Kyiv on the third winter of Russia’s full-scale invasion, finding a populace locked in a temporal stasis. Ukrainian novelist Andrey Kurkov, once hopeful about weekends at his country house, now tells him, “the future is just too far away.” With 60 % of Ukrainians dependent on power stations for heat and hot water, rolling blackouts and lukewarm radiators have become the texture of daily life. Kurkov’s nonfiction trilogy chronicling the war captures soldiers campaigning against gambling addiction and tropical-farm owners coaxing frost-resistant bananas—small acts of impro [TRUNCATED]

Seth Freed Wessler reconstructs the five-day ordeal of Tcherry, a 10-year-old Haitian boy intercepted alone by the U.S. Coast Guard in Florida waters. Detained aboard a cutter with 15 other children, Tcherry spent nights on a steel deck in 50-degree weather, rationed to one bottle of water and two sandwiches a day. Officers logged his name phonetically—“Cherry”—and photographed him for biometric entry into a growing database of child migrants. When a sister ship finally transferred the group to Bahamian authorities, Tcherry’s mother in Canada received a two-line WhatsApp: “They took him back.” [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova revisits Oliver Sacks’s posthumous essay collection “The River of Consciousness,” focusing on his meditation on the 100-millisecond lag between external event and conscious perception. Using neurological case studies of patients who experience temporal “standstills,” Sacks argues that perception is not passive reception but an active creative act—a coalition of neural assemblies that must cross an intensity threshold to reach awareness. This reframes consciousness as a mirror reflecting the perceiver’s own history and desires rather than a transparent window onto the world. Popova [TRUNCATED]

wearable_ai.findings: Friend AI Pendant (MyTabAI, Inc) – $99 pre-order, 15-hour battery, always-listening voice companion that texts you throughout the day; $7.9 M raised, shipping "Spring 2026".  Audria – discreet behind-the-ear voice computer that proactively listens and offers contextual reminders; founded 2025, 7-person team, Delaware/India.  Compass – SF-based necklace that turns conversations into reminders & sum [TRUNCATED]

Humy.ai – 1,200+ interactive AI historical figures + 90 teacher tools for English/History; auto-graded debates, essays, simulations; FERPA/COPPA compliant, 60 k teachers.  Twee – rapid English-teacher generator for questions, dialogues, vocab, levelled texts; 8-person Hallandale/Israel team, 309 k monthly visits.  immersionED – game-based US History curriculum with "Pick Your Path" adaptive simula [TRUNCATED]

Nirva – jewelry-grade AI pendant that journals your life, builds a "living library of memories"; 4-person SF startup, 6 CES 2026 awards.  Ganance Heir – modular sensor that clips to any watch/bracelet, adds activity tracking & haptics; 9-person Chicago team, 1 k pre-orders shipped.  BleeqUp Ranger – AI sports glasses with 4-in-1 action cam, open-ear audio, smart nav, intercom; 7-person Hong Kong/C [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Two Hundred and Fifty Years of Complicated Commemorations
newyorker.dek: Jelani Cobb on observing the Declaration of Independence on previous landmark anniversaries—in the wake of the Civil War, the Vietnam War, Watergate—and why we are unlikely to see Donald Trump presiding over nuanced celebrations of our history this July 4th.
newyorker.text:
Commemorations often tell us as much about the times in which they are being held as they do about the events they are commemorating. The two-hundred-and-fiftieth anniversary of the adoption of the Declaration of Independence, this July 4th, falls at a moment when the nation is being led by a twice-impeached President amid a widely recognized crisis of democracy—last Wednesday, the Supreme Court’s ruling in Louisiana v. Callais further weakened an already enfeebled Voting Rights Act—and in a social climate whose volatility might be measured by acts of political violence. The most dramatic recent example came with the frantic apprehension of an armed man in the hotel where the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner was being held. The alleged gunman fortunately did not reach the ballroom where the President and the other attendees were gathered, but he has been charged with an assassination attempt—the third made against Donald Trump in two years. This is the backdrop against which our recollections of the nation’s origins are taking place.
Commemoration has been a complicated undertaking in this country from the start. On July 4, 1826, President John Quincy Adams decided to forgo making a major speech and instead rode by carriage in a parade to the Capitol, where he listened to celebratory remarks and a reading of the Declaration. He would later find out that two of his predecessors—his father, John Adams, and Thomas Jefferson—had died that day. Once fierce rivals, the two men were responsible for the country’s first peaceful transfer of power between parties, after Jefferson and his Democratic-Republican Party defeated Adams and the Federalists in the election of 1800.
The nation’s centennial, in 1876, arrived in the wake of a civil war that had left some seven hundred thousand Americans dead. The Declaration’s insurrectionist contention—that people, when unjustly provoked, have the right to dissolve their government—hung heavily in a country that had just witnessed the eleven states of the Confederacy make the same argument. That year, President Ulysses S. Grant, who had commanded the Union forces, spoke in his annual address to Congress of the great economic and social progress that the United States had made during its first hundred years. But, though the guns of war were a decade in the past, the nation had not escaped the spectre of conflict. The Presidential election of 1876 pitted the Democrat Samuel J. Tilden against the Republican Rutherford B. Hayes, but disputed returns from Florida, Louisiana, and South Carolina, where there had been violence directed at Black voters, cast the results into doubt. With neither candidate gaining a clear majority in the Electoral College, the election was turned over to a special commission, which declared Hayes the winner. A compromise was then struck, insuring that Hayes could take office in exchange for the Republicans’ promising to cease the federal occupation of the former Confederate states—thereby ending the period known as Reconstruction. Grant was compelled to celebrate the nation’s hundredth anniversary just as its boldest experiment in democracy to date was being dismantled.
The nation’s bicentennial, the only major commemoration in living memory, was framed by the exigencies of the Cold War and by the scarcely healed scars of the Vietnam War, as well as by Watergate and President Richard Nixon’s consequent resignation. President Gerald Ford’s Fourth of July address hit the boilerplate notes of progress and enduring values in a speech so anodyne that his audience might have overlooked the fact that the national festivities were being led by a man who had been elected to neither the Vice-Presidency (Nixon appointed him, in 1973) nor the Presidency (he assumed that office when Nixon resigned). The cumulative lesson of all this recollection is that, in commemorating the past, we may not like all that it calls attention to in the present.
There are other contexts worth considering at this moment. The semiquincentennial inherently underscores the comparative youth of the United States; Italy, Greece, China, and India count their historic legacies in millennia, not centuries. At the same time, the past two hundred and fifty years of popular self-government represent a global milestone: the Declaration of Independence marks the birth of the world’s oldest constitutional democracy. Those divergent truths point to the sober reality that the vast majority of people who have existed on this planet lived under some form of tyranny. The events of July 4, 1776, signalled a partial departure from a miserably well-worn path in human history. In that light, two hundred and fifty years seems like a very long time.
The meaning of the Declaration was not entirely clear at the outset, even to those who wrote or signed it. With the haphazard spelling of the era, the signers refer to themselves in the final paragraph of the document as “Representatives of the united States.” The use of the lowercase with the word “united” suggests that it is serving as an adjective, not as a herald of the new nation’s name. (A subsequent line refers to the confederation as “United Colonies”—there were still things to be ironed out.) Notably, it was not until after the end of the Civil War that American grammar reliably described the United States in the singular: “the United States is” rather than “the United States are.” Other editorial decisions in the founding document were less ambiguous. Jefferson’s first draft contained a hundred-and-sixty-eight-word denunciation of the transatlantic slave trade, which was excised from the final text. A seed of conflict was sown in that moment.
Donald Trump’s personal aversion to admitting fault suggests that we will not likely see commemorations that grapple with the nuanced, complicated nature of the country’s founding and subsequent history this Fourth of July. What is more likely is that, fifty years hence, Americans will observe that this anniversary took place during a polarizing time, but that such circumstances were not unprecedented. One version of the nation’s history anchors itself in the efforts to navigate those tempests, to better the imperfect tools bequeathed to us by imperfect men. This more mature approach to our past recognizes that national greatness does not exist without a simultaneous reckoning with national failure—and that this undertaking, rather than diminishing American standing, is the surest path toward a country where “united” is as much an aspiration as an adjective. ♦

literary_pick.title: The Known World
literary_pick.author: Edward P. Jones
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the 2004 Pulitzer Prize and hailed as one of the best books of the 21st century by The New York Times, Edward P. Jones's debut novel is a masterwork of historical fiction set in antebellum Virginia. It tells the story of Henry Townsend, a former slave who becomes a plantation owner and slaveholder himself. After his death, the plantation begins to unravel as his widow Caldonia grieves and the enslaved people seek freedom. The novel explores the complex and morally intricate world of slavery, weaving together the lives of the freed and the enslaved, and offering a profound understandi [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.title: Iran's New Winter | Christopher de Bellaigue | The New York Review of Books
enriched_articles.text: In 1953 Iran's prime minister, Mohammad Mossadegh, was toppled in a coup planned by MI6 and the CIA and carried out by Iranian army units and hoodlums supportive of Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi. Mossadegh had nationalized Iran's British-run oil industry and allowed Iranians more freedom than they had ever known; the constitutional monarchy he favored was a path between the extremes of royal tyranny and popular radicalism that had beset the country for the previous three quarters of a century. In t

enriched_articles.title: Who needs AI text-generation when there's Erasmus of Rotterdam | Aeon Essays
enriched_articles.text: The Renaissance scholar and educator Erasmus of Rotterdam opens his polemical treatise The Ciceronian (1528) by describing the utterly dysfunctional writing process of a character named Nosoponus. The Ciceronian is structured as a dialogue, with two mature writers, Bulephorus and Hypologus, trying to talk Nosoponus out of his paralysing obsession with stylistic perfection. Nosoponus explains that it would take him weeks of fruitless writing and rewriting to produce a casual letter in which he as

enriched_articles.title: The many lives of Eurasian daimonology | Aeon Essays
enriched_articles.text: Back in my student days in late 1970s Paris, one of my favourite walks would take me to the fabulous Père Lachaise cemetery near the eastern edge of the French capital. A miniature city of monumental tombs and crypts in a lush garden setting, it has long been 'home' to the remains of no small number of the very special dead: Molière, Oscar Wilde, Colette, Sarah Bernhardt, Chopin … and Jim Morrison. Morrison's gravesite was always part of my itinerary, because it was a gathering place for a colou

enriched_articles.title: Hope out of chaos: how the dark era of Trump is creating a new approach to global politics
enriched_articles.text: There is cautious optimism that 'a majority of multilateralists' may be able to enduringly reshape the world order. Antonio de Aguiar Patriota, Brazil's ambassador to London, had no difficulty joining the dystopians describing the modern world in a recent speech, a world suffering from 'global warming and environmental degradation, multiple conflicts, rising military budgets, disregard for international law and international humanitarian law, disruptions to trade, erosion of democratic governanc

enriched_articles.title: MWC 2026: Best wearables and AI hardware announcements
enriched_articles.text: After several days of packed halls at MWC 2026—where the Wareable team dodged everything from back-flipping robots to King Felipe VI—the Barcelona show is officially in the books. There were undeniably fewer hardware launches in wearables than in previous years, with much of the focus this time fixed on 6G and AI infrastructure. Yet, the telco-first nature of the conference didn't stop a few exciting wearable tech announcements from breaking through. While most brands are clearly waiting for the
